item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I previously downvoted back when it was in its "wholesome" state, but now with the added maliciousness I've changed it to an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was halfway through with the feeling of "man, I'm really not liking how this scp seems to be leaning on abusers being told they're forgiven and the abused being asked for forgiveness they justifiably shouldn't be asked to give" and then the last line hit.

I understand that you rewrote this once from heartwarming to sinister, but sinister definitely works better as far as a narrative goes, and the overall message.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if both parties recieved a letter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the wholesomeness, but I do think that the new, more ominous ending text fits better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, could it be based around the things people don't say. Asking forgiveness can be as hard as forgiving sometimes, so the notes could be greasing the skids a little bit in that way.

Alternatively (And based on the "let me have my fun" bit), it could absolutely be malicious in forcing an injured person to approach the one that harmed them and lead to further harm.

Either way, it would be nice to know how the next interaction between the two goes over in order to better understand the nature of the SCP itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wholeheartedly liked the article. I can understand why people struggle with the “bad” people getting forgiveness from the people they’ve wronged, but in real life it’s hard to dismiss a loved family member or a longstanding relationship. It is always more complicated than just completely cutting those people out and, from a psychological standpoint, it may not be healthy to either.

Another thing I liked, was it left the reader questioning on how the instances of the SCP got the notes. In my head canon, it knows what the hurt party wants to say to the offending party but has not worked up the courage to say, or they are still hurt and refuse to say what they want and need to say.

The tone could be a little more subtle and clinical, but I know rewrites are coming. Overall I would love to read more about the SCP maybe in tales about the Homophobic mother or the wife beater. Those types of tales could be heartwarming too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops sorry I meant for that to be it’s own post. I’m new to the site and I’m still learning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry meant for this to be it’s own post

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The addendum legitimately made me rethink all of this SCP's previous actions. Especially in light of the D-class's suicide, this thing is definitely a lot more sinister than I initially assumed. Succinct, well written, with a twist I never would've seen coming. Thank you for making this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wholesome and cute. Such harmless and beneficial SCP. Hope to see more like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy the idea of something malicious being behind nice-sounding messages. It's a simple but clever enough subversion and works well as a riff on the more schmaltzy stories on the SCP wiki or elsewhere. The process in getting there shows badly, though; this point being accidentally stumbled upon and edited in after the article was finished (from what I can gather from the comments) is reflected in the twist feeling thrown in at the end and never expanded upon, with no convincing buildup beforehand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think a lot of the comments are saying this is bad because some of the recipients did irredeemable things. I think it's less about the emotions of the recipients, and more about those of the "senders": in addition to whatever tricks the lilies might be playing, it's completely normal to miss your grandma even when she rejects you for being homophobic, and unfortunately completely normal to have conflicting feelings about your abusive husband. Feelings don't work rationally, and I think the two notes for horrible people read more "look how badly your actions have affected the people who loved and cared about you" than "you're fine and deserve to go back to the way things were." Having an irrational urge to forgive someone who's hurt you irredeemably is its own awful thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one feels better with someone behind it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda feel this would be better as a benevolent or at least benign scip, the "malevolent thing that knows your secret pain" type is a lot more common here than a well-meaning, naive type, additionally that last line feels as grafted on as the comment chain in here reveals it to be.

item.title: Potential Error
item.content:encoded:
"Subject diagnosed as suffering from social anxiety after dropping our of college due to financial issues. Handwriting matched subject's father."

Did you mean to say out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's r0se not r0ses smh

+1, glad the therapy suggestion i gave you morphed further as i really liked this! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll try doing a genuinely wholesome scip another day, preferably after the workshop so I learn how not to mess up execution.

But I cannot thank you enough for sticking around long enough to watch this piece develop. With that said, thank you very much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I sincerely wish the wholesome route could’ve stuck it’s landing. I loved the premise of it. Nonetheless, the revision you’ve made provides an intriguing twist that is well set up. Changed to +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of communicating with the dead is something i will shelve for later use, so many thanks for that.

Scip has since been revised to reflect the more sadistic aspect of the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would be an honor to have your help in fixing up the messes this scip made

I'll credit you as co-author once you're done.

Thank you for the offer!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alternatively, allowing the grieving party receive messages actually written by deceased others, from some sort of afterlife, would make the article's message seem more genuine.

It'll also tap into the unused symbolism of death in Lycoris radiata.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OP I'd be more than willing to help rewrite aspects of this if you would have me. Granted, this article isn't very long, but I think that turning this into a malicious entity could be a better route for this.

When I read the first few paragraphs, I was hooked- I'm personally a sucker for heartwarming stuff. I didn't think that it was the entity writing the notes, either. At the end though, it felt missing.

As other commenters have said, this has pretty big potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few people have already touched on the conflicting intended message and actual consequences of the anomaly and I tend to agree with them. Like many, I've had major issues with a family member. I don't believe forgiveness was ever on the table in my case, even after their death. Blood isn't as strong a bond as its made to be.

Having the anomaly try to maliciously manipulate people would work way better than what I assume was an attempt at delivering a wholesome message.

Alternatively, allowing the grieving party receive messages actually written by deceased others, from some sort of afterlife, would make the article's message seem more genuine. I'd recommend having fewer examples of its effects but developing them more or making them more sympathetic.

edit: I've changed my rating to +1 following revisions made to the article. Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To both of you, thank you for your inputs.

I suppose the downfall of this scip was my own naivety. I hadn't expected there to be the possibility of this being a subversion of "you are forgiven" idea. I recognize the apologies in of themselves were pretty cheap, and was planning on fixing them after the seminar later this week.

I half-heartedly considered reposting this as actual subversion after revising everything but the immersion's been broken, and I'm sure that would constitute as breaking some rule regarding downvotes. In any case, I'll leave it up for now to see how it goes. If this does get deleted, I'll consider revising this to be more sappy to push forward the idea of a malicious entity screwing with our emotions, or try to make it less hamfisted in an attempt to actually make something wholesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Compound the fact that the apologies aren't actually coming from the people in question but the anomaly, this means they're totally false, implying that the recipients would be mislead to reach out.

This. My biggest problem with this is that it jams a gear into independent systems. The note wasn't written by either side, which means the offender never really admitted the mistakes (even if they did to themselves) and the offended unilaterally forced to forgive the offender — to give the offender 'second chance' when there's no real evidence that they deserve any. Like stormbreath said, had the entity been intentionally malicious this would be brilliant, but it's clearly not intended to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of these notes read like "People with legitimate reasons to no longer contact former associates receive fake apologies (or fake forgiveness vice versa)" to the point that I actually thought this was a malicious entity until I read the comments and discovered it was a hamfisted attempt at being heartwarming.

I mean, you've got: a mother who abandoned her child, abusive siblings, a homophobic mother, a wife-beater and an implication that one of the recipients should kill himself. These people need to give a lot better of an apology than "sorry bro". The only two that are legitimate are the orphan girl and the college dropout. Compound the fact that the apologies aren't actually coming from the people in question but the anomaly, this means they're totally false, implying that the recipients would be mislead to reach out.

The two legitimate notes have other issues — I don't know too much about the homeless/orphanage system1 but I'm pretty sure six-year-old girls are not tossed into the general homeless shelter system without a guardian. Usually, they're brought into what CPS-type agency exists in the country — which would mean back to an orphanage or foster home. The first note really doesn't make a lick of logistical sense.

The college dropout is described as suffering from "stress-related dysphoria". That's not really an actual diagnosis and not something the Foundation would use: dysphoria is often a symptom of another mental issue. The Foundation would use a more specific term.

But really, I'm downvoting because I legitimately thought the entity was malicious and the fact I thought that means you fucked up your message big time. If that was intentional, I'd upvote.

Footnotes
1. Largely because there aren't many countries were orphanages even exist anymore

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First and foremost, thank you so much ch00bakka for taking the time to express your opinion. And thank you as well, anamazingfantasy for taking the time to respond while I was asleep.

My Own Response
My Own Response

anamazingfantasy hit the key point here: the object isn't what the person's opinion is. It's the notion that there's still a chance for them to redeem themselves.

For the first lady, her opinions are reprehensible. I know of one person who disagrees and he's since been banned for homophobic remarks. At the same time, however, you'll notice that the focus isn't on her, per se, but her children. Her children just want their mother back. Her opinions are opinions. They sting, they're disgusting, but it's never stated that she actually abused or kicked them out because they were LGBT.

Here lies the first point of this scip: the need to be in a group. If there's anything I've learned over the years, it's that being alone is the most terrifying feeling in the world. We, as social creatures, are designed to interact. Staying alone is a horrible experience. It's why sitting time out in the corner was a usually decent form of punishment and humiliation, while solitary in prison is just awful.

People are more likely to express their opinions while they have people around them with the same ideology. It's why the Nazis and the KKK are so vocal. They know they have people around them to support them. When you take away this social aspect, people become frustrated and confused. They lash out at loved ones who don't feel the same way, they act defensive - it's all a part of human nature.

The woman, in this scenario, is in her own solitary. Unable to adapt to the changing times, she finds herself arguing with others over who gets to sleep with what gender. She's confused, because she comes from a time where such opinions were the norm, and she's frightened - because she's not used to this. Lashing out, she deepens her own hole, which culminates in her dying alone. The withered plant is to show the death of possible redemption.

People won't change their opinions just because you want them to. People don't like to be wrong. You can't expect someone to act how you want them to act by flat out confronting them. Hell, I wish that was possible so that "truthers" would stop humiliating families. By showing support, you remind people that they're not weird, allowing them to gradually lower their defenses and confide in you. At that point, it's easier convince them to see the issue from your point of view.

An analogy would be throwing a siege versus inviting the enemy commander to drink tea, in front of everyone, and becoming his friend so he warmly invites you into his home the next time you meet up.

Now for the second one: again, I completely agree with you that he's a piece of shit. This section is unique because we notice clashing between the woman's thoughts and her actions: the notes claim her thoughts still love him but there's no action to indicate she'll ever fix it.

Here, we focus not on the person's past, but active attempt to redeem himself. He's penning letters, begging for a second chance. He knows he's done shit, and he wants to redeem himself.

The problem however, is that we notice that this conflict will not resolve. The woman explicitly states that even she has limits to her patience. Here we feel bad for the man, not because he's a shit stain, but because we know how this cycle goes: fits of mania, possible psychosis, possible return to alcoholism - everything he swears not to do, he will do. We pity the man not because he doesn't get an answer but because we know he won't escape his cycle of violence.

The Foundation, if you read it again, never explicitly helped them. They just administered amnestics. Who knows, maybe they were F-class amnestics, and they lived on without remembering either person exists. It also raises another question, "Would an instance still appear if neither person remembers the dispute?"

As anamazingfantasy stated, they're still humans. Considering how often we do give second chances (although the American criminal system is a terrible example), there's no reason that such people can't be given a chance to redeem themselves.

I mean, we have all these heartwarming tales about D-Class, who supposedly did something so horrific that they were forced onto death row, yet they do such heroic tales that they either become immortalized in tales or even be pardoned by the Foundation. If such people can be given a second chance, I don't see why an asshole with a shitty opinion can't be given the chance to reevaluate their life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will have to disagree on this point.

Long Response
------------------

I would like to interject my own anecdotal account on these matters, but I will refrain from such. I'm a firm believer that actions make the person, but this doesn't mean it has to permanently define them. While the actions of the two characters are atrocious, they're still human (much like in real life). I don't want to engage in controversy, but I think the main point of this article is that love can be shown to anyone, despite previous failings and horrible mistakes. It's the notion that second chances are still viable (a beautiful theme, which is why I want to see this succeed.) Example: A majority of incarcerated criminals are ashamed of the actions they chose, and wish for a second chance, redemption. Certainly, you can agree that such is possible for these individuals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this piece is trying to make me feel bad for a shitty old lady who hates her gay son and a dude who gets drunk and beats his wife. i don't know what possessed you to inject some heartwarming pathos into the lives of these two awful people, but seriously buddy, don't do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Its simple, and it tells some interesting stories. Good Job +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes its angle of pathos clear within the first couple of sentences, and got somehow less subtle after that. It's hard to be heartwarmed when you know someone trying to warm your heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of Zyn’s soup from dad SCP, although with much less evocative execution. I feel like the pieces are here but they’re not fitting together yet. Concurring that the idea has legs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. There's real, tangible potential here, and I'd hate to see it disappear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's absolutely understandable. I'll work on a revised version as soon as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as it pains me, I feel as though I must downvote on the principle of execution. The delivery of the message just doesn’t sit well: clinical tone is lax in some parts, information could probably be condensed, and there’s an explicit desire to make the reader empathetic instead of subtly hooking us.

Nevertheless, this is a premise that I love and want to see flourish on the main list. Continue to work on this, I urge you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone fixed it while I was away

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh crap

Thankfully someone fixed it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my opinion this is a great SCP, you just have to swap out the XXXXs. But either way, you got an upvote from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You gotta swap out the XXXXs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ungodly sweet, mildly childish, and possibly saccharine.

Miraculously, it's still alive and isn't causing controversy. Thanks to the folks in IRC like AbsentmindedNihilist, TRutherford, r0se, Jiwoahn, Lordmep, and anyone else who looked in the critters section that I lost record of.

Deadly Bread is to thanks for changing the Xs into actual numbers.

Poor execution hindered this piece, and a revision is being done as I'm typing this. With any luck, following the seminar later this week, I'll make this less "please feel emotions" and more evocative, internet willing.

Edit: as of 4/27/19, the focus of this scip has shifted from wholesomeness to "asshole tries to fuck with emotions."
